title: I
html:
I

On a June afternoon, not long after five o‚Äôclock, the family were sitting before the ‚Äúportal‚Äù in the garden, where they had drunk coffee. They had pulled the rustic furniture outside, for it was too close in the whitewashed garden house, with its tall mirror decorated with painted birds and its varnished folding doors, which were really not folding doors at all and had only painted latches.

The Consul, his wife, Tony, Tom, and Clothilde sat in a half-circle around the table, which was laid with its usual shining service. Christian, sitting a little to one side, conned the second oration of Cicero against Catiline. He looked unhappy. The Consul smoked his cigar and read the Advertiser. His wife had let her embroidery fall into her lap and sat smiling at little Clara; the child, with Ida Jungmann, was looking for violets in the grass-plot. Tony, her head propped on both hands, was deep in Hoffman‚Äôs Serapion Brethren, while Tom tickled her in the back of the neck with a grass-blade, an attention which she very wisely ignored. And Clothilde, looking thin and old-maidish in her flowered cotton frock, was reading a story called ‚ÄúBlind, Deaf, Dumb, and Still Happy.‚Äù As she read, she scraped up the biscuit-crumbs carefully with all five fingers from the cloth and ate them.

A few white clouds stood motionless in the slowly paling sky. The small town garden, with its carefully laid-out paths and beds, looked gay and tidy in the afternoon sun. The scent of the mignonette borders floated up now and then.

‚ÄúWell, Tom,‚Äù said the Consul expansively, and took the cigar out of his mouth, ‚Äúwe are arranging that rye sale I told you about, with van Henkdom and Company.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat is he giving?‚Äù Tom asked with interest, ceasing to tickle Tony.

‚ÄúSixty thaler for a thousand kiloÔªø‚Äînot bad, eh?‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs very good.‚Äù Tom knew this was excellent business.

‚ÄúTony, your position is not comme il faut,‚Äù remarked the Frau Consul. Whereat Tony, without raising her eyes from her book, took one elbow off the table.

‚ÄúNever mind,‚Äù Tom said. ‚ÄúShe can sit how she likes, she will always be Tony Buddenbrook. Tilda and she are certainly the beauties of the family.‚Äù

Clothilde was astonished almost to death. ‚ÄúGood gracious, Tom,‚Äù she said. It was inconceivable how she could drawl out the syllables. Tony bore the jeer in silence. It was never any use, Tom was more than a match for her. He could always get the last word and have the laugh on his side. Her nostrils dilated a little, and she shrugged her shoulders. But when the Consul‚Äôs wife began to talk of the coming dance at the house of Consul Huneus, and let fall something about new patent leather shoes, Tony took the other elbow off the table and displayed a lively interest.

‚ÄúYou keep talking and talking,‚Äù complained Christian fretfully, ‚Äúand I‚Äôm having such a hard time. I wish I were a business man.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, you‚Äôre always wanting something different,‚Äù said Tom. Anton came across the garden with a card on his tray. They all looked at him expectantly.

‚ÄúGr√ºnlich, Agent,‚Äù read the Consul. ‚ÄúHe is from HamburgÔªø‚Äîan agreeable man, and well recommended, the son of a clergyman. I have business dealings with him. There is a piece of business now.Ôªø‚ÄîIs it all right, Betsy, if I ask him to come out here?‚Äù

A middle-sized man, his head thrust a little forward of his body, carrying his hat and stick in one hand, came across the garden. He was some two-and-thirty years old; he wore a fuzzy greenish-yellow suit with a long-skirted coat, and grey worsted gloves. His face, beneath the sparse light hair, was rosy and smiling; but there was an undeniable wart on one side of his nose. His chin and upper lip were smooth-shaven; he wore long, drooping side-whiskers, in the English fashion, and these adornments were conspicuously golden-yellow in colour. Even at a distance, he began making obsequious gestures with his broad-brimmed grey hat, and as he drew near he took one last very long step, and arrived describing a half-circle with the upper part of his body, by this means bowing to them all at once.

‚ÄúI am afraid I am disturbing the family circle,‚Äù he said in a soft voice, with the utmost delicacy of manner. ‚ÄúYou are conversing, you are indulging in literary pursuitsÔªø‚ÄîI must really beg your pardon for my intrusion.‚Äù

‚ÄúBy no means, my dear Herr Gr√ºnlich,‚Äù said the Consul. He and his sons got up and shook hands with the stranger. ‚ÄúYou are very welcome. I am delighted to see you outside the office and in my family circle. Herr Gr√ºnlich, BetsyÔªø‚Äîa friend of mine and a keen man of business. This is my daughter Antonie, and my niece Clothilde. Thomas you know already, and this is my second son, Christian, in High School.‚Äù Herr Gr√ºnlich responded to each name with an inclination of the body.

‚ÄúI must repeat,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúthat I have no desire to intrude. I came on business. If the Herr Consul would be so good as to take a walk with me round the gardensÔªø‚Äî‚Äù The Consul‚Äôs wife answered: ‚ÄúIt will give us pleasure to have you sit down with us for a little before you begin to talk business with my husband. Do sit down.‚Äù

‚ÄúA thousand thanks,‚Äù said Herr Gr√ºnlich, apparently quite flattered. He sat down on the edge of the chair which Tom brought, laid his hat and stick on his knees, and settled himself, running his hand over his long beard with a little hemming and hawing, as if to say, ‚ÄúWell, now we‚Äôve got past the introductionÔªø‚Äîwhat next?‚Äù

The Frau Consul began the conversation. ‚ÄúYou live in Hamburg?‚Äù she asked, inclining her head and letting her work fall into her lap.

‚ÄúYes, Frau Consul,‚Äù responded Herr Gr√ºnlich with a fresh bow. ‚ÄúAt least, my house is in Hamburg, but I am on the road a good deal. My business is very flourishingÔªø‚ÄîahemÔªø‚Äîif I may be permitted to say so.‚Äù

The Frau Consul lifted her eyebrows and made respectful motions with her mouth, as if she were saying ‚ÄúAhÔªø‚Äîindeed?‚Äù

‚ÄúCeaseless activity is a condition of my being,‚Äù added he, half turning to the Consul. He coughed again as he noticed that Fr√§ulein Antonie‚Äôs glance rested upon him. She gave him, in fact, the cold, calculating stare with which a maiden measures a strange young manÔªø‚Äîa stare which seems always on the point of passing over into actual contempt.

‚ÄúWe have relatives in Hamburg,‚Äù said she, in order to be saying something.

‚ÄúThe Duchamps,‚Äù explained the Consul. ‚ÄúThe family of my late Mother.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, yes,‚Äù Herr Gr√ºnlich hastened to say. ‚ÄúI have the honour of a slight acquaintance with the family. They are very fine people, in mind and heart. Ahem! This would be a better world if there were more families like them in it. They have religion, benevolence, and genuine piety; in short, they are my ideal of the true Christlike spirit. And in them it is united to a rare degree with a brilliant cosmopolitanism, an elegance, an aristocratic bearing, which I find most attractive, Frau Consul.‚Äù

Tony thought: ‚ÄúHow can he know my Father and Mother so well? He is saying exactly what they like best to hear.‚Äù The Consul responded approvingly, ‚ÄúThe combination is one that is becoming in everybody.‚Äù And the Frau Consul could not resist stretching out her hand to their guest with her sweeping gesture, palm upward, while the bracelets gave a little jingle. ‚ÄúYou speak as though you read my inmost thoughts, dear Herr Gr√ºnlich,‚Äù she said.

Upon which, Herr Gr√ºnlich made another deep bow, settled himself again, stroked his beard, and coughed as if to say: ‚ÄúWell, let us get on.‚Äù

The Frau Consul mentioned the disastrous fire which had swept Hamburg in May of the year 1842. ‚ÄúYes, indeed,‚Äù said Herr Gr√ºnlich, ‚Äútruly a fearful misfortune. A distressing visitation. The loss amounted to one hundred and thirty-five millions, at a rough estimate. I am grateful to Providence that I came off without any loss whatever. The fire raged chiefly in the parishes of St.¬ÝPeter and St.¬ÝNicholas.Ôªø‚ÄîWhat a charming garden!‚Äù he interrupted himself, taking the cigar which the Consul offered. ‚ÄúIt is so large for a town garden, and the beds of colour are magnificent. I confess my weakness for flowers, and for nature in general. Those climbing roses over there trim up the garden uncommonly well.‚Äù He went on, praising the refinement of the location, praising the town itself, praising the Consul‚Äôs cigar. He had a pleasant word for each member of the circle.

‚ÄúMay I venture to inquire what you are reading, Fr√§ulein Antonie?‚Äù he said smiling.

Tony drew her brows together sharply at this, for some reason, and answered without looking at him, ‚ÄúHoffmann‚Äôs Serapion Brethren.‚Äù

‚ÄúReally! He is a wonderful writer, is he not? Ah, pardon meÔªø‚ÄîI forget the name of your younger son, Frau Consul?‚Äù

‚ÄúChristian.‚Äù

‚ÄúA beautiful name. If I may so express myself‚ÄùÔªø‚Äîhere he turned again to the ConsulÔªø‚Äî‚ÄúI like best the names which show that the bearer is a Christian. The name of Johann, I know, is hereditary in your familyÔªø‚Äîa name which always recalls the beloved disciple. My own nameÔªø‚Äîif I may be permitted to mention it,‚Äù he continued, waxing eloquent, ‚Äúis that of most of my forefathersÔªø‚ÄîBendix. It can only be regarded as a shortened form of Benedict. And you, Herr Buddenbrook, are readingÔªø‚Äî? ah, Cicero. The works of this great Roman orator make pretty difficult reading, eh? ‚ÄòQuousque tandemÔªø‚ÄîCatalina‚ÄôÔªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ ahem. Oh, I have not forgotten quite all my Latin.‚Äù

‚ÄúI disagree with my late Father on this point,‚Äù the Consul said. ‚ÄúI have always objected to the perpetual occupation of young heads with Greek and Latin. When there are so many other important subjects, necessary as a preparation for the practical affairs of lifeÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúYou take the words out of my mouth,‚Äù Herr Gr√ºnlich hastened to say. ‚ÄúIt is hard reading, and not by any means always unexceptionableÔªø‚ÄîI forgot to mention that point. Everything else aside, I can recall passages that were positively offensiveÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

There came a pause, and Tony thought ‚ÄúNow it‚Äôs my turn.‚Äù Herr Gr√ºnlich had turned his gaze upon her. And, sure enough: he suddenly started in his chair, made a spasmodic but always highly elegant gesture toward the Frau Consul, and whispered ardently, ‚ÄúPray look, Frau Consul, I beg of you.Ôªø‚ÄîFr√§ulein, I implore you,‚Äù he interrupted himself aloud, just as if Tony could not hear the rest of what he said, ‚Äúto keep in that same position for just a moment. Do you see,‚Äù he began whispering again, ‚Äúhow the sunshine is playing in your daughter‚Äôs hair? Never,‚Äù he said solemnly, as if transported, speaking to nobody in particular, ‚Äúhave I seen more beautiful hair.‚Äù It was as if he were addressing his remarks to God or to his own soul.

The Consul‚Äôs wife smiled, well pleased. The Consul said, ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt be putting notions into the girl‚Äôs head.‚Äù And again Tony drew her brows together without speaking. After a short pause, Herr Gr√ºnlich got up.

‚ÄúBut I won‚Äôt disturb you any longer nowÔªø‚Äîno, Frau Consul, I refuse to disturb you any longer,‚Äù he repeated. ‚ÄúI only came on business, but I could not resistÔªø‚Äîindeed, who could resist you? Now duty calls. May I ask the ConsulÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúI hope I do not need to assure you that it would give us pleasure if you would let us put you up while you are here,‚Äù said the Frau Consul. Herr Gr√ºnlich appeared for the moment struck dumb with gratitude. ‚ÄúFrom my soul I am grateful, Frau Consul,‚Äù he said, and his look was indeed eloquent with emotion. ‚ÄúBut I must not abuse your kindness. I have a couple of rooms at the City of HamburgÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúA couple of rooms,‚Äù thought the Frau ConsulÔªø‚Äîwhich was just what Herr Gr√ºnlich meant her to think.

‚ÄúAnd, in any case,‚Äù he said, as she offered her hand cordially, ‚ÄúI hope we have not seen each other for the last time.‚Äù He kissed her hand, waited a moment for Antonie to extend hersÔªø‚Äîwhich she did not doÔªø‚Äîdescribed another half-circle with his upper torso, made a long step backward and another bow, threw back his head and put his hat on with a flourish, then walked away in company with the Consul.

‚ÄúA pleasant man,‚Äù the Father said later, when he came back and took his place again.

‚ÄúI think he‚Äôs silly,‚Äù Tony permitted herself to remark with some emphasis.

‚ÄúTony! Heavens and earth, what an idea!‚Äù said the Consul‚Äôs wife, displeased. ‚ÄúSuch a Christian young man!‚Äù

‚ÄúSo well brought up, and so cosmopolitan,‚Äù went on the Consul. ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt know what you are talking about.‚Äù He and his wife had a way of taking each other‚Äôs side like this, out of sheer politeness. It made them the more likely to agree.

Christian wrinkled up his long nose and said, ‚ÄúHe was so important. ‚ÄòYou are conversing‚ÄôÔªø‚Äîwhen we weren‚Äôt at all. And the roses over there ‚Äòtrim things up uncommonly.‚Äô He acted some of the time as if he were talking to himself. ‚ÄòI am disturbing you‚ÄôÔªø‚Äî‚ÄòI beg pardon‚ÄôÔªø‚Äî‚ÄòI have never seen more beautiful hair.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù Christian mocked Herr Gr√ºnlich so cleverly that they all had to laugh, even the Consul.

‚ÄúYes, he gave himself too many airs,‚Äù Tony went on. ‚ÄúHe talked the whole time about himselfÔªø‚Äîhis business is good, and he is fond of nature, and he likes such-and-such names, and his name is BendixÔªø‚Äîwhat is all that to us, I‚Äôd like to know? Everything he said was just to spread himself.‚Äù Her voice was growing louder all the time with vexation. ‚ÄúHe said all the very things you like to hear, Mamma and Papa, and he said them just to make a fine impression on you both.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat is no reproach, Tony,‚Äù the Consul said sternly. ‚ÄúEverybody puts his best foot foremost before strangers. We all take care to say what will be pleasant to hear. That is a commonplace.‚Äù

‚ÄúI think he is a good man,‚Äù Clothilde pronounced with drawling serenityÔªø‚Äîshe was the only person in the circle about whom Herr Gr√ºnlich had not troubled himself at all. Thomas refrained from giving an opinion.

‚ÄúEnough,‚Äù concluded the Consul. ‚ÄúHe is a capable, cultured, and energetic Christian man, and you, Tony, should try to bridle your tongueÔªø‚Äîa great girl of eighteen or nineteen years old, like you! And after he was so polite and gallant to you, too. We are all weak creatures; and you, let me say, are one of the last to have a right to throw stones. Tom, we‚Äôll get to work.‚Äù

Pert little Tony muttered to herself ‚ÄúA golden goat‚Äôs beard!‚Äù and scowled as before.
